mnemonics.title: PROTECTION VS SECURITY
mnemonics.content: PROTECTION: Internal control of resource access by legitimate users. SECURITY: External defense against threats. REMEMBER: P for INTERNAL (First letter), S for EXTERNAL (First letter) - P before S in alphabet mirrors internal before external.

mnemonics.title: ACCESS MATRIX
mnemonics.content: S O C K - Subjects (rows) vs Objects (columns) contains Knowledge of rights. S-R-C: Subjects, Rights, Classes - think of classification of rights.

mnemonics.title: DAC MAC RBAC
mnemonics.content: DAC: Discretionary - Owner DECIDES. MAC: Mandatory - system ENFORCES levels. RBAC: Role - job FUNCTION determines access.

mnemonics.title: PRINCIPLE OF LEAST PRIVILEGE
mnemonics.content: LEAST: Limited Elevation Assigned Specifically To users. Only give what is NEEDED - Nothing More.

acronyms.expansion: Access Control List - attached to objects, lists subjects and their rights
acronyms.usage: Used in Windows, UNIX for file permissions

acronyms.expansion: Role-Based Access Control - assigns permissions to roles not users
acronyms.usage: Used in enterprise systems where job functions define access

acronyms.expansion: Mandatory Access Control - system-enforced classification levels
acronyms.usage: Used in military and government systems

acronyms.expansion: Discretionary Access Control - owner decides who accesses resources
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability - three pillars of security
acronyms.usage: Fundamental security goals for any system

acronyms.expansion: Denial of Service - attack that makes system unavailable
Single Sign-On - authenticate once access multiple systems

visualTips.visualization: Draw a grid: ROWS = users/processes, COLUMNS = files/devices. Cell (User A, File 1) = 'RW' means User A can Read/Write File 1. Visualize as a spreadsheet where you look up row then column to find permissions.
Think of concentric circles: Innermost = kernel/most trusted, Middle = system services, Outer = user applications. Each outer circle has fewer privileges than inner ones.

Think of a company org chart: Roles (Manager, Employee, Intern) are the circles. Users are badges assigned to roles. Permissions are attached to role badges, not to individuals. Change roles = change access.
Trojan: Horse Trojan hiding soldiers - disguised as something else. Worm: Worm eating through apple - self-spreading through system. Virus: Biological virus - needs host to replicate.

analogies.analogy: Protection is like internal house rules (don't go into certain rooms), while Security is like the house alarm and security cameras against outsiders.
ACL is like a guest list at a club entrance (check list shows who's allowed). Capability is like an ID card you carry (you have the proof with you).
Like a hotel key card system: Room keys work for certain floors based on your room. You don't get individual keys for everything - your role (guest type) determines access.
Like giving someone a library card that only allows reading (not writing) if they only need to read books. Don't give write access unless they need to add books.
Like a bank vault door - the only way to access the valuable resources (safety deposit boxes) is through this trusted, heavily protected checkpoint.
Like your personal diary - you decide who can read it. You're the owner with discretionary control.
Like military clearance - no matter who you know, if your clearance level is lower, you cannot access certain documents. The system enforces this regardless of who you are.
